overall_feedback: Round 1 P1 items all landed cleanly. Two new issues introduced by the §3.4 paragraph and the new Manhattan-unimodality lemma: (i) spelling regression to -ise/-isa- in two locations (paper otherwise uses Oxford -ize), and (ii) a localised exposition slip inside the new lemma proof (calls phi 'weakly decreasing' when it is min of a decreasing and an increasing function, i.e. unimodal). The proof's conclusion is correct; the sentence is not. No new substantive issues. Score 93/100.

comments.title: New unimodality lemma proof misstates monotonicity of phi
comments.quote: Both arguments of the minimum are continuous and weakly decreasing in \(c\), so \(\phi\) is weakly decreasing.
comments.explanation: The second argument 1 - sum p_v^{(L)}(c) is weakly *increasing* in c (since p_v^{(L)}(c) is weakly decreasing). phi is the min of a decreasing and an increasing function; it is unimodal (peaks at the crossing c*), not 'weakly decreasing'. The proof's subsequent case analysis is correct and uses the right monotonicity directionally, but this descriptive sentence directly contradicts it.
comments.fix: Replace with: 'The first argument is weakly decreasing in c, the second weakly increasing, so phi is unimodal, with maximum at the crossing point c*.'

comments.title: Oxford-UK spelling regression: -ise/-isa- in new §3.4 paragraph
comments.quote: the same threshold parameterisation: the identified mean is a linear functional of \(p\) optimised over the same threshold-indexed box-simplex slice ... the mean bounds are sharp on the same threshold parameterisation but are implemented as a grid optimisation over the threshold.
comments.explanation: Paper otherwise uses Oxford UK conventions: -ize/-ization (minimize, maximize, normalize, parameterization, optimization, characterization appear throughout) combined with -our/-re/-ce (favour, centre, behaviour). These two new sentences in §3.4 use 'parameterisation', 'optimised', 'optimisation' (-ise/-isa-), creating a local inconsistency.

comments.title: Cross-reference in abstract
comments.quote: two further count-loss rules, Hamming and Chebyshev distance, violate it, and Section~\ref{sec:other-rules} takes them up as the boundary of the approach.
comments.explanation: Abstracts are often consumed detached from the body (indexers, abstracting services). Including a \ref to an in-body section is unconventional and may render awkwardly outside the PDF. Consider naming the section directly ('the discussion takes them up...') or dropping the cross-ref.
comments.fix: Replace 'Section~\ref{sec:other-rules}' with 'the discussion' or 'a closing section'. Keep the in-body \refs unchanged.

comments.title: Missing comma before non-restrictive 'which' in abstract
comments.quote: with a closed-form crossover in the number of positive-report coordinates which a design comparison then locates as a crossover in the Dirichlet concentration parameter.
comments.explanation: The 'which' clause is non-restrictive (it adds rather than restricts) and should be set off by a comma.
comments.fix: Insert comma: '...positive-report coordinates, which a design comparison...'

comments.title: Lemma label 'manhattan-unimodal' but title 'Single-equation characterization'
comments.explanation: The lemma is labelled 'unimodal' but its title and content are framed as a single-equation/single-bisection characterisation. Unimodality is a step inside the proof, not the lemma's headline statement. Cosmetic only — no semantic issue.
comments.fix: Optional: rename label to 'lem:manhattan-single-eq' or 'lem:manhattan-bound', and adjust the two \ref calls in 03_scoring_rules.tex lines 275–276. Or accept the misalignment.
